problem_statement.Title: TypeError in pytest assertion rewriter when first expression of test file is a number
problem_statement.Description: When a test file starts with a numeric literal (e.g., `123`) as its first expression, pytest's assertion rewriter crashes with `TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable` during test collection. The rewriter incorrectly attempts to treat the numeric value as a docstring.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. Incorrect assumption: Code in `AssertionRewriter.run()` at line 681 assumes any `ast.Constant` node at module start is a string docstring, passing `item.value.value` directly to `is_rewrite_disabled()` without verifying its type. 2. Incorrect assumption: The `is_rewrite_disabled()` method at line 745-746 expects a string parameter (as indicated by its signature `def is_rewrite_disabled(docstring: str) -> bool`) but receives the raw constant value from line 681, which can be an integer. 3. Type check logic gap: Line 678 checks `isinstance(item.value, ast.Constant)` which is insufficient because `ast.Constant` represents ANY constant type (int, str, bool, None) — not just strings.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file where the first expression is a standalone numeric literal, e.g., a file containing only `123` as its first line
Run pytest on the file using `pytest -k xxx` or simply collect the test
Observe the TypeError during test collection
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest successfully collects and runs tests regardless of what the first expression in the test file is, treating only actual string docstrings as docstrings and ignoring other constant values.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest crashes with `TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable` during test collection, failing to load the test file.

problem_statement.Title: Module imported twice under import-mode=importlib causing class initialization to be ineffective
problem_statement.Description: When using --import-mode=importlib, pytest creates duplicate module entries in sys.modules for the same logical module (pmxbot.logging). This results in core.initialize() initializing one Logger class instance while test code references a different Logger class that lacks the initialized store attribute.
RootCause: insert_missing_modules in src/_pytest/pathlib.py (lines 650-653) creates dummy placeholder modules using ModuleType(name, doc) without setting __spec__. This causes failures when other code (like the assertion rewriter at src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py:128-130) accesses module.__spec__ and expects it to be non-None.

import_path in src/_pytest/pathlib.py (line 537) assigns sys.modules[module_name] = mod without checking if a module with that name already exists in sys.modules. This allows the same module to be imported multiple times, creating different module objects.

module_name_from_path in src/_pytest/pathlib.py (lines 606-624) generates module names based on the path relative to root, not the actual package structure. These generated names may not match real package names (e.g., pmxbot.logging), causing the same physical module to be imported under different keys in sys.modules.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The Logger class initialized by core.initialize() should be the same Logger class referenced by test code, meaning Logger.store should be accessible.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After core.initialize() runs, logging.Logger.store raises AttributeError because the test code references a different Logger class object than the one initialized. This is confirmed by sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging returning False.

problem_statement.Title: Unhelpful failure messages for all()/any() assertions with iterables
problem_statement.Description: When using `all()` or `any()` with generator expressions or list comprehensions in assertions, the pytest assertion re-writer displays unhelpful failure messages showing `<generator object>` or `[False, False, ...]` instead of indicating which specific element caused the failure. This makes debugging difficult as users cannot identify which element failed the predicate.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. The `AssertionRewriter` class in `src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py` lacks special handling for `all()` and `any()` built-in functions - both `visit_Call_35` (lines 967-992) and `visit_Call_legacy` (lines 1000-1028) treat all function calls identically with generic handling that produces `<generator object>` in the explanation. 2. No helper function exists to detect all/any calls (needed around line 549) so the rewriter cannot distinguish these calls from regular function calls. 3. The explanation formula `"%s(%s)" % (func_expl, ", ".join(arg_expls))` produces representations like `<generator object>` or `[False, False, ...]` for iterables rather than tracking individual element values.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with a predicate function, e.g., `def is_even(n): return n % 2 == 0`
Write a test using `assert all(is_even(number) for number in [1, 3, 5])`
Run pytest on the test file
Observe the assertion error shows `<generator object>` instead of indicating that `is_even(1)` failed
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The assertion failure message should indicate which specific element caused the failure (e.g., showing `is_even(1)` or similar), allowing users to identify which value in the iterable failed the predicate, analogous to how a for loop assertion would report the failing element.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The assertion failure message displays `<generator object test_all_even.<locals>.<genexpr> at 0x...>` for generator expressions, or `[False, False, ...]` for list comprehensions, with no indication of which element in the iterable caused the failure.

problem_statement.Title: pytest --fixtures does not display fixture scope
problem_statement.Description: When running `pytest --fixtures`, the displayed fixture information does not include the scope of each fixture. Users must consult documentation or source code to determine whether a fixture is session, package, module, class, or function scoped.
RootCause: 1. _showfixtures_main in src/_pytest/python.py (lines 1345-1348) assumes scope information is not useful for display — the funcargspec is built using only argname and bestrel, never incorporating fixturedef.scope.

2. _show_fixtures_per_test's write_fixture helper in src/_pytest/python.py (lines 1261-1265) assumes scope information is not useful for display — the funcargspec construction ignores fixture_def.scope.
3. The valid scope values ("session", "package", "module", "class", "function") are defined in src/_pytest/fixtures.py at line 675 but are never displayed to users.
StepsToReproduce: Run `pytest --fixtures` in a project with pytest installed
Observe that no scope information is displayed for any fixture
Note that to determine fixture scope, users must look at source code or documentation
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The `--fixtures` output should display the scope for each fixture (e.g., [session], [module], [class], [function]) alongside the fixture name and location.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The `--fixtures` command displays fixture names, locations, and docstrings, but omits scope information entirely. Even fixtures like `tmpdir` which have `scope='session'` are not labeled with their scope.

problem_statement.Title: Default logging format lacks module name making it difficult to identify log sources
problem_statement.Description: The default logging format only includes the filename without the module/logger name, making it difficult to identify which module produces log messages when multiple modules generate logging output. Users cannot distinguish between different loggers sharing the same filename.
RootCause: 1. Wrong assumption: The default format only needs %(filename)s without the logger/module name component. Location: src/_pytest/logging.py:18 where DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" lacks %(name)s.

2. This constant is referenced by all three logging output paths (log_format at line 111, log_file_format at line 156, log_cli_format at line 440), propagating the missing module name to all logging contexts.

3. The correct format relationship should be: %(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s - placing the module/logger name before the filename, with no fixed-width padding on filename since module names vary in length.
StepsToReproduce: 1. Run pytest with a test that produces logging output from multiple modules
2. Observe the default log output format showing only filenames without module path
3. Note that multiple modules with the same filename cannot be distinguished
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Log messages should display the full logger name (e.g., 'django.db.backends') followed by the filename, allowing clear identification of which module produced each log message.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Log messages display only the filename (e.g., 'utils.py') without the module/logger name prefix, making it difficult to identify the source module when multiple modules generate logging output.

problem_statement.Title: str() on pytest.raises context variable returns only first line of exception message
problem_statement.Description: When using pytest.raises() context manager, calling str() on the captured exception returns only the first line of the multi-line message instead of the complete message, unlike normal Python exception catching where str(exception) returns the full message.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. ExceptionInfo.__str__() in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (lines 537-542) constructs a ReprFileLocation object and converts it to string. 2. ReprFileLocation.toterminal() in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (lines 947-955) explicitly truncates the exception message at the first newline character: it finds the first '
' in the message and slices the message to only that first line (msg = msg[:i]). This design was intended to produce editor-friendly output showing only the first line with file:line prefix, but it differs from normal Python exception handling where str(exception) returns the complete message without truncation.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest with a test that raises a multi-line LookupError inside pytest.raises()
Call str() on the pytest.raises context variable
Observe that only the first line of the exception message is displayed
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: str() on the pytest.raises context variable should return the full exception message, matching the behavior of normal Python exception catching where str(exception) returns the complete message.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: str() on the pytest.raises context variable prints only the first line of the exception message (e.g., 'A'), truncating the rest of the multi-line message. Users must call str(e.value) to get the full message.

hints_text:
> Any chance the behavior could be changed to eliminate this gotcha?

What do you suggest?

Proxying through to the exceptions `__str__`?
Hi @fiendish,

Indeed this is a bit confusing.

Currently `ExceptionInfo` objects (which is `pytest.raises` returns to the context manager) implements `__str__` like this:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/9f8b566ea976df3a3ea16f74b56dd6d4909b84ee/src/_pytest/_code/code.py#L537-L542

I don't see much use for this, I would rather it didn't implement `__str__` at all and let `__repr__` take over, which would show something like:

```
<ExceptionInfo LookupError tb=10>
```

Which makes it more obvious that this is not what the user intended with `str(e)` probably.

So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Thoughts?

Also, @fiendish which Python version are you using?
> So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Makes sense to me.


Python 3.7.3

My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.
> My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.

I understand, but I think it is better to be explicit here, because users might use `print(e)` to see what `e` is, assume it is the exception value, and then get confused later when it actually isn't (an `isinstance` check or accessing `e.args`).
+1 for deleting the current `__str__` implementation
-1 for proxying it to the underlying `e.value`

the `ExceptionInfo` object is not the exception and anything that makes it look more like the exception is just going to add to the confusion

problem_statement.Title: Pytest assertion diff shows ASCII ordinals instead of byte values for byte strings
problem_statement.Description: When pytest's assertion rewriting compares byte strings of different lengths, the error message displays ASCII ordinal values (e.g., 52, 49) instead of the actual byte characters (e.g., b'4', b'1'). This makes the comparison output confusing and harder to interpret.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. In `_compare_eq_sequence` at src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:263, the code uses `left[i]` and `right[i]` to display differing elements, assuming indexing returns a sequence element of the same type. For bytes and bytearray objects in Python 3, indexing returns an integer (the ASCII value) rather than a bytes object of length 1. The formula `left[i]` returns int for bytes, but the code expects it to return bytes.
2. At src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:271 and :275, the code uses `left[len_right]` and `right[len_left]` to get extra items when one sequence is longer, with the same incorrect assumption. When `right = b"42"` and `len_left = 0`, the expression `right[len_left]` returns `52` (ASCII of '4'), not `b'4'`. The formula `right[len_left]` returns int for bytes, but the code expects bytes.
3. The function `issequence` at src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:85 returns True for bytes because bytes is a `collections.abc.Sequence` but not str, routing byte strings through `_compare_eq_sequence` where the indexing bug manifests.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with: def test_b(): assert b'' == b'42'
Run pytest on the test file
Observe the error message showing ASCII ordinal 52 instead of b'4'
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The assertion error message should display actual byte values (e.g., b'4') when showing the first extra item in the longer byte string, making it clear which byte characters differ between the two byte strings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The assertion error message displays ASCII ordinal values (e.g., 'Right contains more items, first extra item: 52') instead of the corresponding byte characters (e.g., 'first extra item: b'4''), making it difficult to understand which byte values are actually different.

hints_text: hmmm yes, this ~kinda makes sense as `bytes` objects are sequences of integers -- we should maybe just omit the "contains more items" messaging for bytes objects?

problem_statement.Title: JUnit XML reports missing hostname and timestamp attributes in testsuite element
problem_statement.Description: When pytest generates JUnit XML reports, the resulting XML is missing the `hostname` and `timestamp` attributes on the `<testsuite>` element. These attributes are present in standard JUnit XML reports but are not being populated by pytest.
RootCause: Missing timestamp implementation - the timestamp attribute is not computed or added to the testsuite element in `src/_pytest/junitxml.py`. The `pytest_sessionstart` hook records `self.suite_start_time = time.time()` but this value is only used to calculate total test duration (line 648-669), not to generate an ISO 8601 timestamp string for the XML output.

Missing hostname implementation - the hostname attribute is not computed or added to the testsuite element in `src/_pytest/junitxml.py`. There is no code to retrieve the system hostname (e.g., via socket.getfqdn()) and no hostname attribute is passed when constructing the Junit.testsuite() element (lines 660-669).
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest with JUnit XML output enabled (e.g., `pytest --junit-xml=report.xml`)
Open the generated XML report
Inspect the `<testsuite>` element attributes
Observe that `hostname` and `timestamp` attributes are missing entirely
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML report should include `hostname` attribute containing the machine's hostname (e.g., 'Anass-MacBook-Pro.local') and `timestamp` attribute containing the test run start time in ISO 8601 format (e.g., '2019-04-22T10:32:27'), matching the standard JUnit XML format used by other test frameworks.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The `<testsuite>` element in pytest's generated JUnit XML reports lacks the `hostname` and `timestamp` attributes that are expected in standard JUnit XML format. These attributes are entirely absent from the generated XML, not present but empty.

problem_statement.Title: INTERNALERROR when exception in __repr__ for object with custom __getattribute__
problem_statement.Description: When a test fails with an exception and the object being tested has both a custom __getattribute__ that raises on any attribute access and a __repr__ that also raises, pytest crashes with an INTERNALERROR instead of properly reporting the original test failure.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. The function _format_repr_exception in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py at line 23 uses obj.__class__.__name__ which assumes obj.__class__ can be accessed safely. This triggers the __getattribute__ lookup protocol on objects that override it, causing a second exception to propagate when the object has a custom __getattribute__ that raises on any attribute access. The correct pattern is type(obj).__name__ which bypasses the attribute lookup protocol entirely. 2. Line 12 in _try_repr_or_str and line 89 in _format both correctly use type(obj).__name__, showing the inconsistency in the code where line 23 was missed.
StepsToReproduce: Create a class with a __getattribute__ method that raises on any attribute access
Add a __repr__ method to the same class that also raises
Create an instance of this class and access an attribute on it in a test
Run the test with pytest
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should display a proper test failure report showing the original exception from the test, despite the object's __repr__ raising.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest crashes with an INTERNALERROR and displays 'RuntimeError: No active exception to reraise' instead of showing the test failure.

problem_statement.Description: When a test fixture changes the current working directory (via os.chdir() or monkeypatch.chdir()), pytest displays incorrect relative file paths in tracebacks. The path shows '../test_path_error.py' instead of 'test_path_error.py', making it impossible for editors to navigate to the error location.
RootCause: FormattedExcinfo._makepath() in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (line 791-799) incorrectly assumes py.path.local().bestrelpath() computes paths from the pytest invocation directory. It actually uses os.getcwd(), which changes when a fixture modifies the working directory. The relative path should be computed using config.invocation_dir instead.

_getreprcrash() in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (line 563-567) incorrectly bypasses _makepath entirely, creating ReprFileLocation directly from entry.frame.code.raw.co_filename. This also displays incorrect paths when the working directory changes.

FormattedExcinfo class in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (line 644-658) incorrectly assumes it doesn't need invocation directory context. The class only tracks display style parameters but doesn't store the path resolution context needed for accurate relative path computation.

ExceptionInfo.getrepr() in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (line 569) incorrectly assumes it can work without the invocation directory. It creates FormattedExcinfo but doesn't pass invocation directory context, which is required when tests modify CWD.

_repr_failure_py in src/_pytest/nodes.py (line 364-370) incorrectly assumes os.getcwd() check is sufficient for determining path display. It checks the new CWD (after fixture changes) rather than the original pytest invocation directory, causing the abspath flag to be set incorrectly.
StepsToReproduce: 1. Create a test file test_path_error.py with a fixture that changes directory using os.chdir() or monkeypatch.chdir()
2. In the test, assert False to trigger an AssertionError
3. Run pytest and observe the displayed file path in the traceback
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The test file path displayed in the traceback should be relative to the original pytest invocation directory (e.g., 'test_path_error.py:29'), regardless of directory changes made by fixtures.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The traceback displays the test file path relative to the new working directory set by the fixture, showing a relative path with '../' prefix (e.g., '../test_path_error.py:29') instead of the correct path relative to the original directory.

problem_statement.Title: Incorrect caching of skipif/xfail string condition evaluation ignores module globals
problem_statement.Description: The skipif/xfail string condition evaluation uses a cache key based only on the expression string, ignoring the globals dictionary that contains module-specific variables. This causes incorrect skipping behavior when the same expression is used across different test modules with different global variable values.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. Wrong assumption: cached_eval in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (lines 21-31) uses only the expression string as the cache key (line 30: evalcache[expr]), ignoring the globals dictionary parameter d. 2. Wrong assumption: evalcache_key at src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py line 18 creates a store key that persists across test items, causing cross-contamination between modules. 3. Wrong assumption: MarkEvaluator._istrue at src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (lines 99-101) accepts the faulty caching behavior by calling cached_eval with globals that are subsequently ignored.
StepsToReproduce: Create test_module_1.py with: skip = True and @pytest.mark.skipif('skip') on a test function
Create test_module_2.py with: skip = False and @pytest.mark.skipif('skip') on a test function
Run pytest test_module_1.py test_module_2.py
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: test_should_skip in test_module_1 should be skipped (because skip=True), and test_should_not_skip in test_module_2 should run and fail (because skip=False).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Both tests are skipped, regardless of the different skip values in their respective module globals.

problem_statement.Title: skipping: --runxfail breaks pytest.mark.skip location reporting
problem_statement.Description: When `@pytest.mark.skip` or `@pytest.mark.skipif` marks are used with the `--runxfail` option, the skip location is incorrectly reported as an internal pytest location (e.g., `src/_pytest/skipping.py:238`) instead of the actual test file location.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. In `src/_pytest/skipping.py` method `pytest_runtest_makereport`, the elif branch at lines 273-274 with condition `item.config.option.runxfail` executes `pass` when `--runxfail` is enabled, which short-circuits the entire elif chain and prevents subsequent branches from executing. 2. The skip location fix at lines 294-305 is positioned as an elif branch that depends on all previous conditions being False, including the runxfail condition. This means when `--runxfail` is enabled, the skip location fix does not run because the chain has already been short-circuited. The condition `item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True) and rep.skipped and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple` correctly identifies when a test was skipped by mark, but it never gets evaluated when runxfail is active.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with `@pytest.mark.skip` decorator on a test function
Run pytest with `-rs --runxfail` flags
Observe the skip location in the output
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The skip location should report the test file and line number where the skip mark is applied (e.g., `test_it.py:3`), matching the behavior when running without `--runxfail`.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The skip location incorrectly reports an internal pytest location (e.g., `src/_pytest/skipping.py:238`) as the skip location instead of the actual test file location.

problem_statement.Title: Dynamically added xfail marker no longer treated as xfail in pytest 6.x
problem_statement.Description: When an xfail marker is added dynamically during test execution using `request.node.add_marker(mark)`, pytest 6.x treats the failing test as a regular failure instead of marking it as xfailed. In pytest 5.x, this same code correctly treated the test as xfail.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1. The function `pytest_runtest_call` in `src/_pytest/skipping.py` (lines 247-257) evaluates xfail markers only BEFORE the test runs (before `yield`), assuming markers are static and present before test execution. 2. The code stores the xfail evaluation result in `item._store[xfailed_key]` before the test executes and never re-evaluates after the test runs. 3. When a marker is added dynamically via `request.node.add_marker()` during test execution (after yield), the stored value remains unchanged and does not reflect the newly added marker.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with: import pytest; def test_xfail_test(request): mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason='xfail'); request.node.add_marker(mark); assert 0
Run pytest with: pytest -rsx test_foo.py
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The test should be marked as xfailed with reason 'xfail' and produce an 'XFAIL' result in the test summary.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The test fails with an AssertionError and produces an 'F' result in the test summary, treating the dynamically added xfail marker as if it were not present.

problem_statement.Title: tmpdir creation fails when username contains illegal characters for directory names
problem_statement.Description: Temporary directory creation fails when the username returned by `getpass.getuser()` contains characters that are illegal in directory names, such as backslash. On Windows with SSH connections, usernames may include domain prefixes (e.g., `contoso\john_doe`), causing the backslash to be interpreted as a path separator.
RootCause: 1. The `get_user()` function at src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:151-159 returns the raw username from `getpass.getuser()` without sanitizing directory-illegal characters. When the username contains characters like backslash (e.g., `contoso\john_doe`), the backslash is interpreted as a path separator when used in directory creation at line 117. This is the same pattern already used at line 193 in `_mk_tmp()` for test name sanitization: `re.sub(r"[\W]", "_", name)`.

2. The `getbasetemp()` method at src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:117 uses the unsanitized username in `f"pytest-of-{user}"` when constructing the base temporary directory path. When the username contains path separators like backslash, the constructed path becomes a nested directory that does not exist, causing `FileNotFoundError`.
StepsToReproduce: Open a command prompt on Windows
Set a username containing a backslash: `set LOGNAME=contoso\john_doe`
Run pytest with a test that uses tmpdir: `py.test test_tmp.py`
Observe the FileNotFoundError: [WinError 3] The system cannot find the path specified
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should successfully create temporary directories for test isolation regardless of whether the username contains directory-illegal characters.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: FileNotFoundError is raised when attempting to create temporary directories because the username with a backslash (e.g., `contoso\john_doe`) creates a nested path that does not exist.

problem_statement.Title: Module-level skip functionality is not discoverable and error message is misleading
problem_statement.Description: When users attempt to skip an entire test module at module level using pytest.skip() without the allow_module_level parameter, they receive an error message that suggests using pytestmark as a workaround. However, pytestmark does not prevent syntax errors from occurring when Python version-specific syntax is used in the module (e.g., positional-only parameters from Python 3.8 running on 3.7). The error message does not mention that skip() accepts an allow_module_level parameter which is the correct imperative solution.
RootCause: 1. Incorrect assumption: The error message at src/_pytest/python.py:611-615 assumes pytestmark is a complete solution for module-level skipping, but pytestmark cannot prevent syntax errors when Python version-specific syntax is parsed on older Python versions
2. Missing guidance: The error message does not mention allow_module_level=True as an option when using pytest.skip() at module level, despite this parameter being the correct solution

3. API gap: No public convenience function (e.g., skip_module()) exists to make module-level skipping more discoverable, unlike pytest.importorskip() which correctly uses allow_module_level=True at src/_pytest/outcomes.py:217
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with Python 3.8+ syntax (e.g., def foo(a, /, b):)
Add pytest.skip() at module level without allow_module_level parameter to skip the test on older Python versions
Run pytest on Python 3.7 or earlier
Observe the error message suggesting pytestmark as the solution, which will fail when the 3.8 syntax is parsed
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Users should be able to skip an entire test module at module level using a straightforward API, and the error message should guide users to the correct solution (allow_module_level=True or a convenience function) rather than an ineffective workaround.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When using pytest.skip() at module level without allow_module_level=True, the error message states 'To skip a module use pytestmark' but this workaround does not prevent syntax errors from being raised during module parsing when the module contains version-specific syntax.

Hi @omry,

Thanks for raising this.

Definitely we should improve that message. 

> Explain skip with allow_module_level in the error message. this seems in conflict with the spirit of the message.

I'm 👍 on this. 2 is also good, but because `allow_module_level` already exists and is part of the public API, I don't think introducing a new API will really help, better to improve the docs of what we already have.

Perhaps improve the message to something like this:

```
Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.
```

I think we can drop the `pytestmark` remark from there, it is not skip-specific and passing `allow_module_level` already accomplishes the same.

Thanks @nicoddemus.

> Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

This sounds clearer.
Can you give a bit of context of why the message is there in the first place?
It sounds like we should be able to automatically detect if this is skipping a test or skipping the entire module (based on the fact that we can issue the warning).

Maybe this is addressing some past confusion, or we want to push people toward `pytest.mark.skip[if]`, but if we can detect it automatically - we can also deprecate allow_module_level and make `skip()` do the right thing based on the context it's used in.
> Maybe this is addressing some past confusion

That's exactly it, people would use `@pytest.skip` instead of `@pytest.mark.skip` and skip the whole module:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2338#issuecomment-290324255

For that reason we don't really want to automatically detect things, but want users to explicitly pass that flag which proves they are not doing it by accident.

Original issue: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/607
Having looked at the links, I think the alternative API to skip a module is more appealing.
Here is a proposed end state:

1. pytest.skip_module is introduced, can be used to skip a module.
2. pytest.skip() is only legal inside of a test. If called outside of a test, an error message is issues.
Example:

> pytest.skip should only be used inside tests. To skip a module use pytest.skip_module. To completely skip a test function or a test class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

problem_statement.Title: pytest displays extra decorator code in assertion error output on Python 3.9
problem_statement.Description: When running pytest on Python 3.9, assertion error output incorrectly includes extra code lines (specifically a decorator `@t` defined inside the test function) that are not part of the failing assertion. This same test on Python 3.7 displays only the relevant assertion lines without the extra decorator code.
RootCause: 1. Core bug in `get_statement_startend2` (src/_pytest/_code/source.py, line 152): The statement boundary calculation uses `x.lineno - 1` unconditionally for all AST statement nodes, assuming `lineno` always points to the actual statement start line. In Python 3.9+, decorated FunctionDef/AsyncFunctionDef/ClassDef nodes have `lineno` pointing to the decorator line (per PEP 570), not the def/class line, causing incorrect boundaries.

2. Downstream impact on boundary calculation: Due to the incorrect lineno values for decorated definitions, the bisect_right algorithm at line 159 places statement boundaries at decorator lines rather than def/class lines, causing later code (like the decorated inner function) to be included in the display range for earlier statements (like the failing assertion).

3. Propagation through getstatementrange_ast (src/_pytest/_code/source.py, lines 168-212): This function calls get_statement_startend2 at line 182 and uses the returned boundaries directly without applying any correction for decorated definitions, passing the incorrect boundaries downstream.

4. Propagation through getsource (src/_pytest/_code/code.py, lines 243-267): This function relies on getstatementrange_ast for determining the `end` boundary at lines 259-261. Because the boundary is incorrectly calculated due to the decorator lineno issue, the source slice returned at line 267 includes extra lines beyond the intended statement range.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file with a fixture that returns 1
Create a test function that uses the fixture
Add a first assertion that will fail: `assert foo == (3 + 2) * (6 + 9)`
Define an inner function inside the test with a decorator: `@t\ndef inner():\n    return 2`
Add a second assertion after the inner function
Run the test with Python 3.9 and observe extra `@t` decorator appears in first assertion error output
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Assertion error output should display only the failing assertion line and its immediate context, without including unrelated code such as decorator definitions that appear later in the test function.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In Python 3.9, the assertion error for the first assertion incorrectly includes the decorator `@t` from an inner function defined later in the test, displaying extra code lines that are not part of the failing assertion. Python 3.7 does not exhibit this behavior.
